summary: DeVault argues that C remains his favorite language because of its fundamental simplicity, despite acknowledging its flaws. He outlines specific coding constraints he follows to write good C, then explains how C's simplicity maps language features closely to CPU behavior, making performance and assembly output predictable. He praises C's seamless integration with assemblers and linkers, its unmatched portability across platforms, and the deliberate conservatism of its feature evolution. While conceding that C needs better generics, pattern matching, and memory safety, he concludes that no replacement language has matched C's elegant simplicity without becoming a kitchen sink.
key_points: C's simplicity means language features map closely to CPU behavior, letting experts predict assembly output and performance characteristics
C's conservative approach to new features (up to 10 years from proposal to shipping) produces stability that faster-moving languages like Rust lack
C's 1:1 symbol mapping to object files makes linking and cross-language interop uniquely straightforward
C is the most portable language in the world — every viable platform has a C compiler, and most other languages were ultimately implemented in C
C's real problems are lack of generics, no pattern matching, fragile memory management, and undefined behavior — but replacements overshoot by adding too much
money_quotes: C is my favorite language, though I acknowledge that it has its warts.
The ultimate goal of any system should be to attain the simplest solution for the problems it faces.
The lifetime of a feature in Rust, for example, from proposal to shipping is generally 0 to 6 months. The same process in C can take up to 10 years.
A C expert could roughly predict the assembly code produced by their compiler for any given C function.
There's a reason every language has a means of writing C bindings, but not generally C++ bindings.
Every operating system on every architecture has a C compiler, and they weren't really considered a viable platform until it did.
Once you have a C compiler you generally have everything else, because everything else was either written in C or was written in a language that was implemented in C.
I would love to see a language that fixes these problems without trying to be the kitchen sink, too.
key_insight: C's enduring value lies in its radical simplicity — features that map directly to hardware, predictable performance, and unmatched portability — and any worthy successor must fix C's flaws without sacrificing that simplicity.
